employee_and_department_information.department: Cook County Department of Facilities Management
employee_and_department_information.title: Building Maintenance Engineer II

previous_disciplinary_actions_table.reasons_for_discipline: Failure to follow lockout/tagout procedures while servicing an air handling unit, resulting in a minor equipment jam and potential safety hazard. Employee received safety re-training and was advised that further violations could lead to suspension.

Unexcused early departure from shift (left the worksite approximately 90 minutes before scheduled end time without notifying a supervisor) and failure to respond to two phone calls from on-call supervisor during that period.

date_of_discipline.reasons_for_discipline: Failure to follow lockout/tagout procedures while servicing an air handling unit, resulting in a minor equipment jam and potential safety hazard. Employee received safety re-training and was advised that further violations could lead to suspension.
Unexcused early departure from shift and failure to respond to supervisor phone calls as an on-call maintenance engineer.

reasons_for_discipline.reasons_for_discipline: Documented violation of established safety protocols (lockout/tagout) during maintenance on air handling equipment at the George N. Leighton Criminal Courthouse. No injury occurred, but the incident created a significant risk to the employee and co-workers.

Pattern of disregard for attendance and communication expectations culminating in an unapproved early departure during a snow emergency, when maintenance coverage was critical for boiler monitoring and snow-melt systems.

basis_for_recommended_action.basis_for_recommended_disciplinary_actio:
This recommendation for a fifteen (15) calendar day suspension without pay for Marcus D. Holloway, Building Maintenance Engineer II, is based on a pattern of misconduct and poor judgment culminating in a serious incident on 2025-11-18 that disrupted operations at the Cook County Juvenile Temporary Detention Center (JTDC) and created unacceptable safety and security risks.

1. Summary of the November 18, 2025 Incident
On Tuesday, 2025-11-18, Mr. Holloway was scheduled to work the 7:00 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. shift at the Juvenile Temporary Detention Center as the primary on-site maintenance engineer. His core duties for that day included:
- Monitoring boiler and HVAC systems in advance of a forecasted cold front.
- Completing a scheduled preventive maintenance check on the backup generator in the B-wing.
- Responding to any work orders submitted via the Facilities Management ticketing system.

At approximately 10:15 a.m., a Facilities work order (Ticket #FM-2025-4473) was generated by JTDC staff reporting flickering lights and intermittent power loss in housing Pod B-3. This pod houses juvenile residents, and consistent lighting and power are critical for safety, camera operation, and staff visibility.

Despite being logged as a priority work order and automatically emailed and texted to Mr. Holloway per standard procedure, he did not acknowledge or respond to the ticket. Internal logs show that the ticket was assigned to him via the maintenance system at 10:17 a.m., with a required response time of 30 minutes for an occupied housing unit.

By 11:05 a.m., pod staff again reported worsening power issues, including momentary loss of interior cameras and loss of power to several electrical outlets. Because Mr. Holloway could not be located on the floor, the JTDC shift commander contacted his County-issued cell phone twice (11:09 a.m. and 11:16 a.m.). Phone records indicate both calls went to voicemail, and there was no response.

At approximately 11:25 a.m., the pod experienced a more extended power interruption, and a juvenile resident slipped and fell while staff conducted a rapid relocation of residents to another pod in lower light conditions. While the resident did not sustain serious injury, this incident triggered a formal incident report within JTDC and an internal safety review.

At 11:32 a.m., the Facilities Management on-call supervisor, Ms. Laura J. Benitez, attempted to reach Mr. Holloway by phone and text and received no response. Ms. Benitez then physically went to the mechanical rooms, common areas, and typical work locations to locate him and was unable to do so. Security badge swipe data later confirmed that Mr. Holloway had exited the building through the staff entrance at 10:32 a.m. and did not re-enter until 12:47 p.m., meaning he was off-site for over two hours during his assigned shift without authorization or notice.

When Mr. Holloway returned, he did not immediately report to his supervisor. Instead, he went directly to the mechanical room and began working on a separate, non-urgent task related to filter changes that had been scheduled for later in the week. He did not open or address the urgent power issue ticket until approximately 1:05 p.m., nearly three hours after the initial report.

2. Violation of Policies and Work Rules
The conduct described above violates multiple County and Department of Facilities Management policies, including but not limited to:

- Attendance and Work Hours Policy: Employees are required to remain on-site during their scheduled shift unless they obtain prior approval from their supervisor or are on an authorized break. Mr. Holloway left the facility for approximately two hours without notifying any supervisor, without clocking out, and without documenting his absence in any system.

- Call-In and Communication Expectations: As the assigned on-site maintenance engineer, Mr. Holloway is required to keep his County-issued cell phone on his person, charged, and available, and to respond promptly to calls and messages, especially from supervisory staff and critical facilities such as detention centers. On November 18, he failed to answer multiple calls and texts from both JTDC staff and his supervisor, and did not return those calls in a timely manner.

- Work Order Response Protocol: Priority tickets relating to power, life safety, and security systems in detention facilities are to be treated as urgent and responded to within 30 minutes. Mr. Holloway ignored or failed to review the urgent work order for nearly three hours, despite being the assigned engineer and being scheduled specifically for that location.

- Safety and Security Obligations in a Detention Setting: The Department’s policies and the JTDC Service Level Agreement emphasize that maintenance staff working in detention facilities are part of the safety and security continuum, and any disruption to power, cameras, or environmental controls must be treated as an immediate concern. Leaving the site without notice and failing to respond to multiple attempts at contact directly undermined this obligation.

3. Prior Disciplinary History and Pattern of Behavior
This is not an isolated incident. Mr. Holloway has a documented history of similar, though less severe, issues involving safety procedures and attendance/communication:

- June 14, 2023 – Written Reprimand: Mr. Holloway received a written reprimand for failing to follow lockout/tagout procedures while servicing an air handling unit at the George N. Leighton Criminal Courthouse. Although no one was injured, the incident created a serious potential hazard. At that time, he was provided with refresher training on safety procedures and was explicitly advised in writing that future violations could lead to more serious discipline, including suspension.

- February 9, 2024 – One-Day Suspension Without Pay: Mr. Holloway was suspended for one day after leaving his assigned worksite approximately 90 minutes before the end of his shift during a severe winter weather event, without notifying a supervisor, and failing to answer two calls from the on-call supervisor while he was off-site. In the discipline notice for that suspension, he was again advised that further incidents involving attendance, communication failures, or disregard for safety-related expectations could result in a longer suspension or termination.

Despite these prior corrective measures, including counseling, written reprimand, and a short suspension, Mr. Holloway has not demonstrated sustained improvement in the areas of communication, adherence to procedures, and reliability during critical conditions.

4. Employee’s Explanation
During the investigatory meeting held on 2025-11-25 with union representation present, Mr. Holloway acknowledged that he left the JTDC at approximately 10:30 a.m. on November 18 to "grab some lunch and clear [his] head" after what he described as a stressful morning. He admitted that he did not request permission from Ms. Benitez or any other supervisor and did not inform JTDC staff that he would be leaving the premises.

He further stated that his County-issued phone battery was low and that he left the phone in his locker charging while he went off-site. As a result, he did not receive or respond to calls and messages during that period. When asked why he did not check the work order system or voicemail messages immediately upon returning, he stated that he "wanted to finish the filter work" and "did not realize the pod lights were that serious." This explanation is not consistent with the priority level assigned to the ticket, the multiple phone calls from JTDC staff and his supervisor, and the training he has received regarding detention facility priorities.

Mr. Holloway stated that he did not intend to put anyone at risk and expressed regret about the juvenile’s fall, but he also minimized the seriousness of leaving the site without authorization, suggesting that "people step out for lunch all the time." This indicates a continued lack of understanding of his responsibilities in a high-risk environment and the importance of being reachable and responsive during his shift.

5. Impact on Operations and Risk to the County
Mr. Holloway’s actions had a direct and negative impact on JTDC operations and exposed the County to potential liability:

- Safety Risk to Residents and Staff: The power issues in Pod B-3 affected lighting and cameras, which are essential to maintaining order, preventing fights or self-harm, and allowing staff to supervise residents effectively. The fall suffered by one juvenile resident, while not ultimately resulting in serious injury, underscores the potential for harm when environmental controls are not properly maintained.

- Security and Monitoring Risk: The intermittent power interruptions disrupted camera feeds, which are a critical tool for documenting incidents, deterring misconduct, and ensuring accountability. Any loss of camera coverage in a detention setting is considered a serious security concern.

- Operational Disruption: Staff were required to relocate residents, adjust staffing patterns, and file additional incident reports, taking time and attention away from normal duties. The shift commander had to devote considerable effort to locating Mr. Holloway and coordinating with Facilities Management to address the problem he should have been handling promptly.

- Reputational and Legal Exposure: Incidents in juvenile detention facilities are subject to heightened scrutiny from oversight bodies, advocacy groups, and the public. A preventable power issue that is prolonged due to an employee’s unauthorized absence and failure to respond to urgent communications increases the risk of complaints, investigations, and potential legal claims against the County.

6. Rationale for the Recommended Level of Discipline
Given the seriousness of the November 18 incident, the prior disciplinary history, and the clear written warnings already provided to Mr. Holloway, a fifteen (15) calendar day suspension without pay is warranted and appropriate for the following reasons:

- Progressive Discipline: The County has already employed lesser forms of discipline—a written reprimand followed by a one-day suspension—for related misconduct involving safety procedures and attendance/communication. Those measures did not result in sustained improvement, and the current incident is more serious in both its potential and actual consequences.

- Deterrence and Accountability: A suspension of this length emphasizes to Mr. Holloway, his coworkers, and other staff working in detention facilities that unauthorized absences, failure to respond to critical work orders, and disregard for communication protocols will not be tolerated. Anything less would risk signaling that such behavior is acceptable or will only result in minor consequences.

- Protection of Residents, Staff, and the Public: The County has a heightened duty of care toward juveniles in custody. Ensuring that maintenance staff take their safety and security responsibilities seriously is essential. A significant suspension underscores that the County prioritizes resident safety and operational integrity over individual convenience.

- Opportunity for Rehabilitation: While serious, this recommendation stops short of termination. It provides Mr. Holloway with a final, clear opportunity to correct his behavior. The suspension should be accompanied by a written performance improvement plan upon his return, requiring him to:
  • Complete refresher training on JTDC-specific safety and security protocols.
  • Demonstrate consistent adherence to attendance and call-in procedures for a defined period (e.g., 12 months) with no further incidents.
  • Meet regularly with his supervisor for check-ins regarding workload, communication, and adherence to work order priorities.

7. Conclusion
In light of the facts gathered during the investigation, the prior disciplinary history, the policies violated, and the potential and actual harm resulting from Mr. Holloway’s actions on November 18, 2025, the Department of Facilities Management recommends a fifteen (15) calendar day suspension without pay. This level of discipline is consistent with progressive discipline principles, proportionate to the seriousness of the misconduct, and necessary to protect the safety, security, and operational integrity of the Juvenile Temporary Detention Center and the County as a whole.
